questions.questionMd: In IR significance testing, bootstrap resampling estimates confidence intervals by which procedure?

options.text: Shuffling relevance judgments across queries to simulate a null distribution
Running the retrieval system multiple times with different random seeds and averaging the results
Repeatedly sampling queries with replacement from the evaluation set and recomputing the metric each time
Randomly splitting the document corpus into halves and comparing system scores on each half

questions.explanationMd: Bootstrap resampling relies on simulating the variation in the query population. It does this by randomly drawing queries from the existing query set *with replacement* to create many synthetic evaluation sets, and then recalculating the metric over these sets to build a confidence interval.

questions.questionMd: Which organisation established TREC (Text REtrieval Conference) in 1992 to provide shared benchmarks for IR evaluation?

options.text: ACM (Association for Computing Machinery)
NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology)
IEEE (Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers)
DARPA (Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency)

questions.explanationMd: TREC was established by NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology) in 1992 to encourage research in IR from large text collections by providing a large test collection, uniform scoring procedures, and a forum for organizations to compare their results.

questions.questionMd: A medical literature search system is designed to ensure clinicians find *every* relevant study on a drug interaction. Which evaluation metric is most appropriate?

options.text: NDCG@10, because graded relevance most accurately reflects a clinician's document preferences
MAP, because it rewards retrieving all relevant documents and penalises any that are missed
MRR, because clinicians only need to find the single most authoritative study for each query
P@5, because clinicians typically only read the first five results returned

questions.explanationMd: When the goal is comprehensive coverage (finding *every* relevant document), recall is critical. Mean Average Precision (MAP) accounts for both precision and recall, strictly penalizing the system if it fails to retrieve some of the relevant documents from the corpus.

questions.questionMd: For a single query, the first relevant document appears at rank 3 in the result list. What is the Reciprocal Rank (RR) for this query?
questions.explanationMd: Reciprocal Rank is defined as `1 / rank` of the first relevant document. Since it is at rank 3, the RR is `1 / 3 ≈ 0.33`.

questions.questionMd: The Ideal DCG (IDCG@k) is defined as the DCG@k of which ranking?

options.text: The ranking produced by the best-performing system in the evaluation pool
The ranking that places all irrelevant documents first to establish a worst-case baseline
The ranking that maximizes Precision@k regardless of graded relevance
The theoretically perfect ranking, where all documents are sorted by descending relevance grade

questions.explanationMd: To normalize DCG into NDCG, it must be divided by the maximum possible DCG. This Ideal DCG (IDCG) is calculated by sorting all known relevant documents in the corpus in descending order of their true relevance grade.

questions.questionMd: A system has a high Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUC-PR). What does this indicate about its retrieval behavior?

options.text: The system maintains good Precision across a wide range of Recall levels simultaneously
The system retrieves all relevant documents within the top 10 results
The system has a very high Precision@1 but low Recall across all cutoffs
The system's ranked list is longer than competitors, covering more of the corpus

questions.explanationMd: A high Area Under the PR Curve indicates that as the system retrieves more documents (increasing Recall), it does not sacrifice much Precision. It maintains strong performance across both dimensions.

questions.questionMd: A new retrieval system retrieves a highly relevant document that was not included in the TREC judgment pool. How does this affect the system's measured evaluation score?

options.text: The document is treated as irrelevant because it was never judged, which unfairly penalises the system
The document is excluded from the metric computation, leaving the system's score unchanged
The document receives the highest relevance grade automatically, rewarding novel retrieval
The evaluation is restarted with an expanded pool that includes the new system's results

questions.explanationMd: In standard TREC pooling, any document that falls outside the original evaluation pool is presumed to be irrelevant (score 0). Therefore, novel systems that find unjudged, highly relevant documents are unfairly penalized.

questions.questionMd: A standard IR test collection consists of exactly three required components. Which of the following correctly identifies all three?

options.text: A training set, a validation set, and a held-out test set
An inverted index, a stemmer, and a stop-word list
A fixed corpus, standardized topics, and human relevance judgments (qrels)
A ranked retrieval model, a query parser, and a relevance feedback module

questions.explanationMd: The classic Cranfield paradigm for IR evaluation requires a document collection (corpus), a set of information needs (topics/queries), and relevance judgments (qrels) mapping which documents are relevant to which queries.

questions.questionMd: Which decade saw the introduction of the Cranfield experiments, the first systematic methodology for evaluating information retrieval systems?

options.text: The 1940s
The 1960s
The 1970s
The 1980s

questions.explanationMd: The Cranfield experiments, conducted by Cyril Cleverdon in the 1960s, pioneered the offline evaluation framework of corpus + queries + relevance judgments that remains the standard today.

questions.questionMd: Recall measures which aspect of retrieval performance?

options.text: The fraction of retrieved documents that are relevant
The rank position of the first relevant document in the result list
The average precision at each recall level across multiple queries
The fraction of all relevant documents that were successfully retrieved

questions.explanationMd: Recall is the metric that answers: 'Out of all the relevant documents that exist in the entire collection, what percentage did we manage to find?'

questions.questionMd: Which formula correctly defines Precision in an information retrieval evaluation context?

options.text: |Retrieved| / |Relevant|
|Relevant| / (|Relevant| + |Retrieved|)
|Relevant ∩ Retrieved| / |Relevant|
|Relevant ∩ Retrieved| / |Retrieved|

questions.explanationMd: Precision is the ratio of relevant documents found relative to the total number of documents retrieved. Therefore, it is the intersection of Relevant and Retrieved, divided by the total number Retrieved.

questions.questionMd: A system retrieves 5 documents for a query that has 10 relevant documents, and all 5 retrieved documents are relevant and ranked perfectly. What is the maximum possible AP for this query?

options.text: 1.0, because every retrieved document is relevant and ranked in perfect order
0.75, because partial retrieval is penalised by a fixed 25% reduction in AP
0.5, because the 5 unretrieved relevant documents each contribute 0 to the AP sum
0.0, because MAP requires all relevant documents to be retrieved before assigning any credit

questions.explanationMd: Average Precision (AP) divides the sum of precisions by the *total number of relevant documents in the collection* (which is 10). The 5 retrieved items yield a sum of 5. The 5 unretrieved items add nothing to the sum. Thus, `5 / 10 = 0.5`.

questions.questionMd: Which formula correctly defines the F1 score in terms of Precision (P) and Recall (R)?

options.text: PR / (P + R)
2PR / (P + R)

questions.explanationMd: The F1 score is the harmonic mean of Precision and Recall. The standard algebraic reduction of the harmonic mean of P and R results in `2 * (P * R) / (P + R)`.

questions.questionMd: In Discounted Cumulative Gain (DCG), relevance scores are divided by the log base-2 of `(rank + 1)`. What user behavior does this logarithmic discount model?

options.text: Users read results in random order, so position should have no effect on contribution
Users prefer documents that are only marginally relevant at high ranks over highly relevant documents at low ranks
User attention diminishes at lower ranks, so a relevant document at rank 10 is worth less than one at rank 1
The discount penalizes systems that retrieve more than k documents, keeping result sets short

questions.explanationMd: The position discount function `1 / log2(rank+1)` simulates the reality that users are highly impatient. A user's probability of inspecting a document degrades logarithmically as they scroll further down the SERP (Search Engine Results Page).

questions.questionMd: What must a developer do in code when computing NDCG@k for a query that has no relevant documents in the collection?

options.text: Return 0.0 as a special-case guard, because IDCG=0 makes the formula undefined
Return 1.0, because a system cannot be penalised for a query with no correct answers
Assign each retrieved document a relevance grade of 1 and recompute normally
Exclude that query from the evaluation and reduce Q by 1 in the MAP formula

questions.explanationMd: If there are zero relevant documents for a query, the Ideal DCG (IDCG) is exactly 0. Attempting to calculate NDCG (DCG / IDCG) would cause a divide-by-zero error. Standard evaluation scripts catch this and typically assign 0.0.

questions.questionMd: In a standard TREC qrels file, each line maps a (query_id, doc_id) pair to a relevance label. What does a grade of 0 in a graded qrels file signify?

options.text: The document is partially relevant - useful but not a complete answer
The document was not in the judgment pool and was therefore never assessed
The query was retired and no longer active in the evaluation campaign
The document was judged by a human assessor and found to be irrelevant to the query

questions.explanationMd: In a TREC `qrels` file, an explicit score of 0 means a human assessor actively read the document and concluded it was not relevant to the query. Documents that were never judged are simply absent from the file.

questions.questionMd: Mean Average Precision (MAP) penalizes a system that fails to retrieve some relevant documents. Why?

options.text: MAP is computed only over queries where the system retrieved at least one relevant document, excluding all others
Unretrieved documents are assigned the lowest possible rank, which increases the denominator of the AP formula
MAP applies a log-discount to later ranks, making missing documents proportionally more costly
Relevant documents that are never retrieved contribute a precision of 0 to the AP calculation

questions.explanationMd: The Average Precision calculation divides the sum of precision values by the *total absolute number of relevant documents*. If a relevant document is never retrieved, it adds 0 to the numerator, effectively bringing the entire average down.

questions.questionMd: In TREC relevance judging, Cohen's kappa (κ) values typically fall between 0.6 and 0.8. What does this imply about IR evaluation?

options.text: Agreement is nearly perfect, so evaluation results can be treated as ground truth without reservation
The κ range shows that automated relevance labeling is more reliable than human judgment
Annotators disagree so frequently that per-query results are unreliable and should be discarded
There is substantial but imperfect agreement among assessors

questions.explanationMd: A Cohen's kappa between 0.6 and 0.8 indicates 'substantial agreement'. It acknowledges that while humans naturally disagree on subjective relevance, the agreement is strong enough to reliably evaluate system performance.

questions.questionMd: Three queries yield Average Precision scores of 0.72, 0.45, and 0.38 for a system. What is the MAP?

options.text: 1.550, because MAP sums the AP values without dividing by the number of queries
0.720, because MAP reports the highest AP score across all queries
0.550, computed as the median of the three AP values

questions.explanationMd: Mean Average Precision (MAP) is simply the arithmetic mean (average) of the Average Precision scores computed across all the queries. `(0.72 + 0.45 + 0.38) / 3 = 0.51666...`

questions.questionMd: In TREC-style evaluation, the pooling method is used to reduce annotation cost. What known bias does this approach introduce?

options.text: Pooling forces all systems to retrieve the same top-k documents, reducing result diversity
Annotators judge documents from all queries simultaneously, leading to topic confusion
Documents in the pool receive inflated relevance scores because annotators see too many results at once
Documents outside the pool are treated as irrelevant

questions.explanationMd: Because humans cannot assess millions of documents, only the top-k results from multiple systems are 'pooled' and judged. If a future system retrieves a brilliant relevant document that wasn't in that original pool, standard scoring algorithms treat it as irrelevant.

questions.questionMd: For which type of query is Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR) the most appropriate evaluation metric?

options.text: Medical literature searches requiring comprehensive coverage of all relevant studies
Ad-hoc multi-document queries evaluated over a large corpus with hundreds of relevant documents
E-discovery tasks where both high precision and high recall are legally required
Navigational or single-answer queries where users care only about finding the first correct result

questions.explanationMd: MRR focuses exclusively on the rank of the *first* correct answer. This makes it perfect for factual question answering (e.g., 'What is the capital of France?') or navigational queries (e.g., looking for a specific website), where finding one correct item satisfies the user.

questions.questionMd: A researcher reports p < 0.05 on a Wilcoxon signed-rank test comparing two IR systems. A colleague argues this alone is insufficient to conclude that one system is better. Why is the colleague correct?

options.text: The Wilcoxon test only applies to normally distributed data; using it on MAP scores is methodologically invalid
p < 0.05 confirms the null hypothesis, meaning the systems perform identically on the query set
A Wilcoxon test requires at least 100 queries before p-values become meaningful, regardless of the result
Statistical significance only rules out chance

questions.explanationMd: A p-value < 0.05 only means that the observed difference is unlikely to be due to random noise. It does NOT guarantee that the difference is practically meaningful or large enough (effect size) to justify replacing a system.

questions.questionMd: What is the typical relationship between Precision and Recall as more documents are retrieved?

options.text: Precision stays constant while Recall increases
Recall decreases as Precision increases, because early ranks contain the best results
Both Precision and Recall increase together as more documents are retrieved
Precision tends to decrease as Recall increases, reflecting a fundamental tradeoff

questions.explanationMd: As you cast a wider net to find all the relevant documents (boosting Recall), you inevitably pull in more 'junk' or irrelevant documents, which drives down your Precision. This inverse relationship is a core rule of Information Retrieval.

questions.questionMd: In a Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR) computation, a query returns a ranked list in which no relevant document appears anywhere in the results. What Reciprocal Rank value is assigned to this query?

options.text: 1.0, because the metric defaults to the maximum when the query cannot be penalised further
0.5, as the midpoint value for a partial retrieval failure
The query is excluded from the MRR calculation to avoid distorting the mean
0.0, because no relevant document was found

questions.explanationMd: If there is no relevant document retrieved, the rank of the first relevant document is theoretically infinite. `1 / infinity` converges to `0.0`. The query is given a score of 0 and still contributes to the average.

questions.questionMd: A research team reports that their new ranking model improves NDCG@10 by +0.03 in offline evaluation but observes no improvement in click-through rate during a live A/B test. What does this illustrate?

options.text: That the A/B test sample was too small and the experiment should be repeated with more users
That click-through rate is an unreliable metric and should be replaced by NDCG in live experiments
That the NDCG improvement is not statistically significant and the offline result should be discarded
That offline metric gains and online user behaviour metrics can diverge significantly

questions.explanationMd: Test collections are static and built by expert annotators. Real users behave differently (skimming, UI preferences, fatigue). A core tenet of modern IR is that offline test collection metrics (NDCG) do not perfectly correlate with online user satisfaction (CTR).

questions.questionMd: When constructing an 11-point interpolated Precision-Recall curve, how is interpolated Precision defined at a given recall level r?

options.text: The minimum Precision across all recall levels r' > r, to represent a pessimistic bound
The arithmetic mean of Precision values immediately before and after recall level r
The Precision value recorded the first time recall r is reached during retrieval
The maximum Precision achieved at any recall level r' that is greater than or equal to r

questions.explanationMd: To smooth the jaggedness of standard PR curves, interpolation is applied. The interpolated precision at recall `r` is defined mathematically as the highest precision found at any recall level equal to or greater than `r`.

questions.questionMd: A ranked list has graded relevance scores `[3, 1, 0, 2, 1]` at ranks 1 through 5. What is the approximate DCG@5? *(Using rel_i / log2(i+1))*

options.text: 7.000, computed by summing the raw relevance grades 3+1+0+2+1
3.000, because only the first rank contributes and its grade is 3
2.500, computed as the arithmetic mean of the five relevance grades

questions.explanationMd: DCG evaluates items based on their grade divided by their position discount: `(3 / log2(2)) + (1 / log2(3)) + (0 / log2(4)) + (2 / log2(5)) + (1 / log2(6))`. Mathematically, this equals approximately 4.879.

questions.questionMd: Which statement correctly describes a known limitation of offline IR evaluation using test collections?

options.text: Test collections used in offline evaluation automatically update their qrels as new systems are submitted
Offline NDCG scores are always higher than online CTR metrics because they are computed over larger document sets
Offline evaluation requires live user traffic and is therefore more expensive than online A/B testing
Gains in offline metrics do not always correspond to improvements in online user behavior metrics

questions.explanationMd: Test collections provide a reproducible sandbox, but their rigid relevance judgments often fail to capture the noisy, context-dependent reality of live user interactions (like layout blindness, intent shifts, or UI friction).

questions.questionMd: Why is Normalized DCG (NDCG) preferred over raw DCG when comparing results across multiple queries?

options.text: Raw DCG rewards retrieving more documents, while NDCG penalizes systems that return more than k results
NDCG uses binary relevance labels, which are easier to collect than the graded labels required by raw DCG
NDCG applies a stricter position discount than DCG, making it more sensitive to early retrieval errors
NDCG normalizes by the ideal DCG so all queries map to [0,1]

questions.explanationMd: Some queries inherently have 10 highly relevant documents, while others only have 1. Raw DCG scores vary wildly because of this. By dividing the DCG by the Ideal DCG, the score normalizes to a 0.0–1.0 scale, allowing fair averaging across diverse queries.

questions.questionMd: R-Precision is defined as Precision computed at rank R, where R is which quantity?

options.text: The rank of the first relevant document returned by the system
The value of the fixed cutoff k chosen by the evaluator
The ratio of retrieved documents to total collection size
The total number of relevant documents for that query in the collection

questions.explanationMd: R-Precision looks strictly at the top R documents retrieved, where 'R' dynamically matches the total number of known relevant documents for that specific query in the entire corpus.

questions.questionMd: In the Average Precision formula `AP = (1/R) × Σ P@k`, what does the summation run over?

options.text: The top-k positions specified by the evaluation cutoff
All positions where the retrieved document is irrelevant, as a penalty term
All rank positions from 1 to the total number of retrieved documents
Only the rank positions k where the retrieved document is relevant

questions.explanationMd: Average precision sums the precision values evaluated *only* at the specific ranks where a relevant document is found. If the document at rank `k` is irrelevant, it contributes nothing to the summation step.

questions.questionMd: A search application requires that Recall be weighted more heavily than Precision. Which setting of β in the Fβ formula achieves this?

options.text: β < 1, because smaller β values shift focus away from Precision toward Recall
β = 1, because this balances Precision and Recall and therefore emphasises neither
β = 0, because this setting removes Precision from the calculation entirely
β > 1, because larger β values increase the weight assigned to Recall in the harmonic mean

questions.explanationMd: The generalized F-beta formula scales the importance of Recall by a factor of beta. When `β > 1` (e.g., F2), Recall is favored. When `β < 1` (e.g., F0.5), Precision is favored.

questions.questionMd: A system achieves NDCG@k = 1.0 for a given query. What must be true?

options.text: NDCG@k=1.0 is impossible; values are strictly bounded below 1.0 in practice
The system retrieved exactly k documents, all of which are relevant
The system's DCG@k equals zero, meaning no relevant documents were returned
The system's ranking is identical to the ideal ranking

questions.explanationMd: NDCG is calculated as DCG divided by Ideal DCG. The only mathematical way for this fraction to equal exactly 1.0 is if the system's DCG equals the IDCG—meaning the system perfectly sorted the documents by their true relevance.

questions.questionMd: System A has MAP = 0.42 and System B has MAP = 0.43. A Wilcoxon signed-rank test yields p = 0.21. What is the correct conclusion?

options.text: System B is statistically and practically superior because it has a higher MAP score
The test is invalid because the MAP difference is smaller than the p-value
System A is preferred because p > 0.05 confirms its stability across queries
The difference is not statistically significant

questions.explanationMd: In hypothesis testing, a p-value above the standard alpha threshold (0.05) indicates that we cannot reject the null hypothesis. The +0.01 difference in MAP is likely just random noise.

questions.questionMd: Why is the Wilcoxon signed-rank test generally preferred over the paired t-test for comparing per-query AP score distributions in IR experiments?

options.text: Wilcoxon is always more statistically powerful than the t-test, producing lower p-values on the same data
The paired t-test requires at least 1,000 queries to produce valid results, while Wilcoxon works on any sample size
Wilcoxon directly optimises NDCG, making it more aligned with the evaluation objective than the t-test
Wilcoxon is non-parametric and does not assume that per-query score differences are normally distributed

questions.explanationMd: IR metrics like Average Precision are rarely normally distributed (they often skew heavily or have ceiling effects). The Wilcoxon signed-rank test is non-parametric, meaning it operates safely on ranks rather than requiring an assumption of normal distribution.

questions.questionMd: A colleague claims that P@10 is equivalent to Average Precision (AP). Which statement best explains why this claim is incorrect?

options.text: P@10 is normalized to [0,1] across queries, whereas AP can exceed 1.0
P@10 uses graded relevance scores while AP uses binary relevance only
AP averages over a fixed cutoff of 10, whereas P@10 considers the entire ranked list
P@10 only counts relevant documents in the top 10 regardless of their positions

questions.explanationMd: Precision@10 gives the same score whether all relevant documents are at ranks 1-5 or ranks 6-10. Average Precision (AP), however, is highly sensitive to the exact rank position, heavily rewarding documents that appear earlier.

questions.questionMd: Given the ranked list `R, I, I, R, R` (where R = relevant, I = irrelevant), what is the Average Precision (AP)?
questions.explanationMd: Precision at each relevant rank: Rank 1 (R) = 1/1 = 1.0. Rank 4 (R) = 2/4 = 0.5. Rank 5 (R) = 3/5 = 0.6. Total relevant documents = 3. `AP = (1.0 + 0.5 + 0.6) / 3 = 2.1 / 3 = 0.700`.

questions.questionMd: Using the DCG formula `rel_i / log2(i + 1)`, what is the position discount factor applied to a document at rank 2?

options.text: Exactly 1.00, because rank 2 is still in the top results and receives no discount
Approximately 0.43, because log2(5) ≈ 2.322 and 1/2.322 ≈ 0.43
Exactly 0.50, because rank 2 halves the contribution relative to rank 1
Approximately 0.63, because log2(3) ≈ 1.585 and 1/1.585 ≈ 0.63

questions.explanationMd: For rank `i=2`, the formula requires evaluating `1 / log2(2 + 1)`, which is `1 / log2(3)`. Since `log2(3)` is roughly 1.585, `1 / 1.585` evaluates to approximately 0.63.
